pages.full_text:
water comes up. It's surrounded by steep, smooth rocks & a pale man fell down, landed on his [?foot] & became even paler. He & his wife immediately lit cigarettes.
   We walked back to the van & stopped in a valley by a draughty stream for lunch. Thomas, Chris, John, Grandpa, Grandma & Helen walked across a hill, & Simon drove the rest of us the long way to a farm to meet them. At the end of the walk Thomas, [?Jim], John & Eve forded a stream on stepping stones. The others took the long route back by the road.
   We stopped at shops & farms for milk, but there wasn't any. So we're having milk water with our cereal tomorrow.
   Thomas & Eve & John & Charles had showers in the morning. When we got back to the campsite, Chris, William, Peter & Justin had showers. It almost rained as we had supper at 8.0.
   Sat Mon 30th
   Chris had an extra blanket last night & was finally warm enough, but she decided to leave at 10.30 with Grandpa, Grandma & Aunt Helen, to go home by bus from Kidderminster, because of her bad cold.
   Simon, Thomas, John & Charles have gone off for a walk. Peter is reading, William reversing, Justin playing cards & Eve about to write letters. We have watched more shaving. The men are learning. The [?vicar] daughter [illegible]
pages.notes: Page begins mid-sentence — continuation from previous spread. No handwritten page number visible in top-left corner. 'Sat Mon 30th' appears to be Saturday/Monday 30th — possibly a slip of the pen (two day names written); most likely 'Sat Mon' means a transition from Saturday to Monday, or 'Mon 30th' with 'Sat' written in error. The entry mentions going home from Kidderminster by bus, suggesting a location accessible from Kidderminster (possibly Wales/Shropshire/Worcestershire area). Year cannot be determined from this page alone; no year is written. The 'pale man fell down' passage likely relates to a waterfall or rocky feature visited. 'Jim' could be 'Tim' or another name — uncertain reading. The final line is partly cut off and last word illegible.

pages.full_text:
[Food list, glued-in card, pencil:]
Fri   crepes, pork pies, toms
      [strikethrough: biscuits] [?Banana] [?strawberries]
Sat
am    sausages, salad
      aftr sponge.
Sat
pm    barbeque, buns, toms
      [strikethrough: apples] [strikethrough: pears] [strikethrough: Bananas]
      [?pistacios]
Sun
am    ct. ch., lettuce, [?maion],
      [strikethrough: [?mini] apples], choc bars.
Sun
pm    [?kiche] salad
      [strikethrough: stewed apples], shortbread
      [strikethrough: resp & strawberries] stewed apple
Mon
am    h.b. [?eggs], salad,
      mayonnaise.
      [strikethrough: [?kiwis]] apples [?&] cherries
      [?ripened.]
Mon
pm    cheese, salad
      rice pud.
Tues
am    corned beef,
      dried fr.

[Narrative text, blue ink:]
Simon, Justin,
Thomas & William
played cricket in
the afternoon [?warmer]
John collected
sheeps wool, &
Eve, sheep shit.
After tea, Eve had
a row with [?Att.].
Simon, Eve, Thomas
John & Charles set
off on a circular
route to the
Mountain Centre.
Charles — "what is
a oner." John "A
twooer is a tine, a
threeer a triangle."
At the Mountain
Centre we met up
with Peter & had
delicious ice creams.
After supper, the
boys & Simon played
pages.notes: Page number 42 visible in top-right corner. The left portion of the right page contains a glued-in or separate food/meal plan card written in pencil, listing meals by day (Fri through Tues) with some items struck through (presumably eaten or substituted). The right portion contains blue-ink narrative. 'Mountain Centre' is a named visitor/outdoor centre — location not specified on this page but consistent with Wales/Shropshire context from left page. 'ct. ch.' in food list likely means 'cottage cheese'. 'h.b. eggs' likely means 'hard-boiled eggs'. '[?maion]' could be 'mayo' or 'maion' (abbreviation for mayonnaise). '[?kiche]' is likely 'quiche'. 'Att.' after 'row with' is unclear — could be a person's name or abbreviation. The quoted exchange about 'oner/twooer/threeer/triangle' appears to be a family joke about golf or cricket scoring. Page ends mid-sentence — continues on next spread. Year cannot be determined from this page alone.
